_comment: Geologic time units, oldest first. Times are in Ma (million years ago). Colors follow the official ICS International Chronostratigraphic Chart. To add a unit: copy any entry and fill it in. 'info' fields are free-form — add or remove keys as you like, the panel renders whatever is present. 'images' paths are relative to the site root; missing images render as labeled placeholders.

info.atmosphere: No free oxygen. A hot haze of carbon dioxide, water vapor, nitrogen and volcanic gases. Early on, possibly steam from a magma ocean.
info.terrain: Molten or freshly solidified crust, constant heavy bombardment by asteroids. The Moon forms from a giant impact early in this eon.
info.notable: Oceans likely condense near the end of the eon.

info.atmosphere: Still essentially oxygen-free: methane, CO2, nitrogen. The sky may have been orange with organic haze.
info.terrain: First stable continental cores (cratons) form. Global oceans, scattered volcanic islands.
info.flora: No plants, but life begins: single-celled prokaryotes and, later, photosynthesizing cyanobacteria building stromatolite mounds.
info.notable: Oldest widely accepted fossils (~3.5 billion years) date from this eon.

info.atmosphere: The Great Oxidation Event (~2.4 billion years ago) introduces free oxygen, rusting the oceans' iron into banded iron formations.
info.terrain: Continents grow and assemble into supercontinents (Columbia, Rodinia). At least two 'Snowball Earth' global glaciations.
info.flora: Single cells dominate for most of the eon; complex eukaryotic cells and, at the very end, the first soft-bodied multicellular life (Ediacaran biota).
info.notable: Roughly half of Earth's entire history fits inside this one eon.

info.atmosphere: Oxygen rising but still below modern levels; high CO2 and a greenhouse climate.
info.terrain: Life is confined to the seas; the land is bare rock and microbial crusts. Continents scattered, Gondwana assembling.
info.flora: Marine algae only — no land plants yet.
info.notable: The Cambrian Explosion: almost every major animal body plan appears in the fossil record, including the first eyes and shells.

info.atmosphere: Very high CO2, yet ending in a sharp ice age and mass extinction.
info.terrain: Vast shallow seas flood the continents — prime real estate for reefs and trilobites.
info.flora: First tiny non-vascular plants (liverwort-like) creep onto land.
info.notable: Ends with the first of the 'Big Five' mass extinctions.

info.atmosphere: Climate stabilizes and warms after the Ordovician ice age.
info.terrain: Coral reefs expand; land is being colonized along rivers and coasts.
info.flora: First vascular plants (like Cooksonia) stand a few centimeters tall.
info.notable: First air-breathing animals on land: millipede-like arthropods.

info.atmosphere: Warm greenhouse world; oxygen climbing as forests appear.
info.terrain: The 'Age of Fishes' in the seas; on land, the first true forests and soils transform the landscape.
info.flora: First trees (Archaeopteris) and first seed plants.
info.notable: Fish grow limbs: tetrapods like Tiktaalik haul out of the water.

info.atmosphere: Oxygen peaks around 30–35% — the highest in Earth's history — fueling giant insects.
info.terrain: Endless tropical swamp forests whose buried remains become most of the world's coal.
info.flora: Towering lycopod 'scale trees', horsetails and tree ferns.
info.notable: Dragonflies with 70 cm wingspans; the first reptiles and the amniote egg.

info.atmosphere: Drying and warming as the supercontinent Pangaea locks together; vast deserts in its interior.
info.terrain: One supercontinent, one super-ocean (Panthalassa). Huge inland dunes and salt flats.
info.flora: Seed ferns and early conifers replace the coal swamps.
info.notable: Ends with the Great Dying — the worst mass extinction ever, erasing ~90% of marine species.

info.atmosphere: Hot and dry, with monsoon extremes. CO2 high, oxygen relatively low after the Great Dying.
info.terrain: All land fused into Pangaea, centered on the equator. Vast red-rock deserts inland, conifer woodlands near coasts.
info.flora: Conifers, cycads, ginkgoes and seed ferns. No grass, no flowers — the ground cover is ferns and horsetails.
info.notable: Life rebuilds after the Great Dying. The first dinosaurs, first pterosaurs, first mammals and first turtles all appear here.

info.atmosphere: Warm, humid greenhouse with no polar ice caps. CO2 several times modern levels.
info.terrain: Pangaea splits apart; the young Atlantic Ocean opens. Shallow seas flood continental edges; lush vegetated lowlands.
info.flora: Conifer forests with an understory of ferns, cycads and ginkgoes. Still no flowering plants.
info.notable: The golden age of giant sauropods. First birds (Archaeopteryx) take wing late in the period.

info.atmosphere: Very warm for most of the period — crocodiles near the poles, sea levels up to 200 m higher than today.
info.terrain: Continents drift toward familiar positions. Huge shallow seas split North America and Europe; chalk forms from plankton (hence the name).
info.flora: The revolution: flowering plants appear and spread explosively, co-evolving with pollinating insects. Magnolias, figs and the first broadleaf forests.
info.notable: Ends abruptly 66 million years ago when the Chicxulub asteroid strikes, ending the non-avian dinosaurs.

info.atmosphere: Starts hot (a major temperature spike ~56 Ma), then a long cooling trend; Antarctica freezes over near the end.
info.terrain: India crashes into Asia and the Himalayas begin to rise. Dense forests reach high latitudes early on.
info.flora: Flowering plants and tropical forests dominate; grasses exist but grasslands are not yet widespread.
info.notable: Mammals inherit the Earth: whales return to the sea, bats take to the air, primates take to the trees.

info.atmosphere: Cooling and drying continues; CO2 drops toward modern values.
info.terrain: Continents essentially in modern positions. Grasslands and savannas spread across the world for the first time.
info.flora: The age of grass. Open savannas reshape evolution — grazers get tall teeth, predators get fast.
info.notable: Apes diversify; hominins split from chimpanzees and begin walking upright in Africa.

info.atmosphere: Ice-age world: CO2 and temperature swing with glacial cycles every ~100,000 years.
info.terrain: Ice sheets repeatedly bury the northern continents, sea levels rise and fall by over 100 m, carving today's landscapes.
info.flora: Modern flora; tundra and steppe expand during glacials, forests during interglacials.
info.notable: Homo sapiens appears (~300,000 years ago), spreads worldwide, and starts building timelines about it.
